tweets.text:
DB on the biggest market paradox: 

"Even as futures price in 2026 Fed rate hikes, other assets aren’t pricing in the  usual consequences of a hawkish Fed. If the Fed really is about to restart a hiking cycle, then financial conditions  are remarkably loose. Equities are around record highs, credit spreads  remain tight, and broader risk appetite looks fine. This is quite different to  recent occasions the Fed started hiking, including the last couple of times  in early 2022, and again in late-2015. Indeed, Bloomberg’s financial  conditions index is currently at one of its most accommodative levels in the  last decade.

Moreover, it’s very rare for the Fed to just deliver a token move and then  reverse course, as market pricing implies. Overwhelmingly in recent  decades, the Fed tends to deliver a full-blown hiking cycle once they start  hiking, rather than a simple “one and done”. 

In both respects, market pricing seems paradoxical. Fed rate hikes are  being priced in, but financial conditions remain highly accommodative.  And it would be historically unusual if just one hike occurred, given the Fed  tend to launch into multiple rate hikes once they start."

tweets.text:
A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: 

Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement.

Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. 

Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.

We have now seen a material slowdown in inbound VLCCs, which all but guarantees a slowdown in loading for Saudi, UAE, Iraq, and Kuwait. Even Iran's own loading is slowing due to insufficient tankers returning.

To maintain leverage over the Strait of Hormuz, Iran will have to enforce compliance with the Oman lane, which all but guarantees more conflict in the days/weeks ahead. Powder keg in the making.

BREAKING: Interest expense on US public debt as a % of GDP is up to ~3.2%, the highest since at least the 1970s.

This figure has nearly TRIPLED over the last 5 years.

Over the same period, national defense spending as a % of GDP has fallen to ~3.0%, the lowest since the early 2000s.

As a result, interest expense has exceeded national defense spending for 3 consecutive years, the longest streak in at least 46 years.

Meanwhile, nominal interest payments have risen +$711 billion since 2020, or +140%, to a record annualized rate of $1.22 trillion.

Over the same period, defense spending has increased +$237 billion, or +35%, to a record annualized rate of $923 billion.

America's debt burden is in uncharted territory.

China is now dominating the global auto market:

China's vehicle exports jumped +68.7% YoY in May, to ~930,000.

This is almost +1,100% above levels seen in May 2019.

This comes as new EV exports surged +112.6% YoY, to 424,000, accounting for ~46% of the total.

As a result, China exported a record 8.6 million vehicles in the 12 months ending May.

By comparison, Japan exported just 4.2 million, or -51% fewer, during the same period.

To put this into perspective, China exported just 1.0 million vehicles in 2019, while Japan exported 4.8 million, or +380% more.

China is now the undisputed leader of the global car market.

That said, with the 2026 World Cup live, that mechanic makes sense because it's built for social distribution, and if you're watching the matches anyway, it's probably a good time to pick a market and try the beta.

They're also running a month-long Predict Festival alongside the tournament: make a prediction any day, and you earn a chest; the more days you stay active, the higher the tier climbs, all opening at the end of the month. - China-EU economic ties remain deeply integrated, underscoring the mutual dependence of their supply chains and markets.
- China and EU retain significant scope for collaboration on climate policy, digital trade, and global development as their interests broadly align, amid rising protectionism and a pivotal phase of WTO reform.

tweets.text:
"Satoshi knew."

a16z crypto research partner @ittaia on how Bitcoin solved a 40-year-old academic problem, and how its creator knew it all along:

"It actually took quite a few years for people to realize that this is kind of solving a Byzantine agreement problem… a very core academic problem that has been studied for 40 years."

"In that sense Bitcoin is kind of this huge revolution in how to solve Byzantine agreement. In fact, if you look at early emails from Satoshi Nakamoto, he said the core technical aspect of Bitcoin is solving Byzantine agreement."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
First Principles Ep. 1

tweets.text:
My partner Steve Jurvetson spent his nights and weekends launching rockets. Totally obsessed.

So I told him, "Find us the best rocket company."

Three weeks later he came back.

"The best rocket company is Elon's."

My first reaction: "Isn't he supposed to be running Tesla?" We already had a big investment in Tesla, but we heard him out.

In that meeting, you could feel it. His mission in life was to make humanity multi-planetary. Tesla was a means to an end.

So we became the first money in after Elon's own $100 million and an investment from Founders Fund.

Just before we invested, the first few rockets had exploded on the launch pad..  but then a Falcon 1 lifted off and worked. It launched one small satellite for Malaysia, and then it was retired.

Falcon 9 was the true workhorse, which good customers came for later over time.

Eventually there were contracts everywhere, and Elon Musk began his journey to make us multiplanetary.

A mission like "we're going to Mars" attracts the best engineers in the world. People take a new job to solve a problem they'll tell their grandkids about.

When Kennedy said we choose to go to the moon, the technologies that came out of that effort changed everything on Earth.

Same thing is happening now. Starlink exists because Elon Musk is building to get us to Mars.

If the mission is grand enough, innovations that happen along the way will enhance and may even redefine the business.

Thanks to Bonnie Blockchain for having me on!

tweets.text:
In addition to absolutely crushing the English in the Viking age, the Norwegians also massively owned the English in polar expeditions.

Here is the story of Amundsen v Scott.

In 1910, Roald Amundsen borrowed Fridtjof Nansen’s ship Fram, originally planning an expedition the North Pole. 

At the last minute, Amundsen changed his mind and secretly set his sights on the South Pole instead. He revealed the true destination to his crew only after leaving their last port of call (!).

Amundsen’s approach was defined by meticulous preparation, experience from the Arctic, expert use of skis and dogs for transport, and lightweight equipment.

Scott’s British expedition relied on ponies (lol) that did not survive, man-hauling sledges, and motors that failed in the extreme cold, while carrying heavier scientific equipment.

Amundsen and his team reached the South Pole on 14 December 1911 and returned safely.

Scott arrived about a month later, on 17 January 1912 … only to find the Norwegian flag already flying 🇳🇴

Tragically, Scott and his men perished on the return journey.

In Amundsen’s own words, “Victory awaits him who has everything in order - luck, people call it. Defeat is certain for him who has neglected to take the necessary precautions in time; this is called bad luck.”

Recap of the past 2 weeks in crypto:

- $BTC managed to hold the line at $58k after several breakdown attempts, and despite intense MSTR+STRC FUD leading to both asset dumping hard. Peak FUD seems behind us, STRC bounced from $72 to $90, and BTC is around $64k

- $LIT has been the strongest coin lately, it properly broke out above $2 after the news that it was the official perp DEX of the Robinhood Chain.

- Moderate SOL season with a +25% move on SOLBTC, as well as the ANSEM memecoin pumping from 0 to $400M mcap. FARTCOIN bounced too. $USELESS remains the best looking memecoin

- $SYN squeezed 25X from bottom to top after its repositioning as an options protocol

- $VVV is bleeding since it was announced that Venice raised $70M but in Venice equity

- Memory stocks are getting totally hammered (-30%) and the semis sector is selling off

- $MON is bouncing (... for no reason?)
